Product Design · Analytics Feature

Agencies were managing their clients' social media. But they were flying blind on the competition.

SocialPilot's Competitor Reports — a new analytics feature that let marketing agencies track how competitors perform on Instagram and Facebook, from inside the same tool they already use to manage and report on their clients' content.

Product Designer All UI & interaction design, from scratch
Every screen, every interaction, every component Within SocialPilot's existing design system
Feature scope, IA placement, report builder pattern PM & leadership · Engineering constraints
New capability — MVP Instagram & Facebook at launch
01 Context

What SocialPilot is, and what was missing from it

SocialPilot is a social media management platform used by over 13,500 customers — primarily marketing agencies handling publishing, analytics, and client reporting across all major platforms from a single dashboard. Agencies connect their clients' accounts, schedule content, track performance, and send white-labelled reports — some automatically, every Monday morning, without touching a button.

But one question SocialPilot couldn't answer: what are our competitors doing? How often are they posting? Are their engagement numbers climbing? Did they just run something that went viral? None of that was visible inside the product. Agencies had to find it themselves — which meant leaving the tool entirely.

"Competitor benchmarking was one of the most-requested features across our user base. Users were naming Sprout Social and Hootsuite as reasons they were considering switching. This wasn't just a product gap — it was a retention problem."

02 The User

The person doing this work every week

Not a data analyst. Not a strategist with a dedicated research budget. A social media manager at an agency, responsible for 8 to 20 client accounts at once, expected to deliver both the posts and the insights that justify them.

S
The Agency Social Media Manager
Managing 8–20 client social accounts · Reporting to clients weekly or monthly

Every Monday morning: one tab for SocialPilot analytics, one for the competitor's Instagram page — manually counting posts, screenshotting follower counts. A Socialinsider trial for the bigger clients. A spreadsheet tying it all together. The data was publicly available. The effort to retrieve it was entirely manual.

"I need to tell my client why their engagement dropped last month. The first thing I have to check is whether a competitor just went viral — but I have to do that in a completely separate tool."

Real feature request — on file
From A SocialPilot user
Subject Feature request
Feature Request: Competitor Tracking and Brand Mention Tracking

I want to be able to monitor competitors' posts, engagement metrics, and audience growth across platforms like Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, TikTok, and YouTube. I also want to be able to monitor mentions of your brand and competitors across platforms like X, Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, Pinterest, and web sources, even if they don't tag us. If you could provide competitive social media analytics, including benchmarking, post and profile monitoring, and detection of promoted posts, that wouldn't hurt, lol.

↗

This request was typical of the volume we received — ambitious in scope (6 platforms, brand mentions, promoted post detection), but with a clear core need: stop making me leave SocialPilot to understand what my competitors are doing. It directly informed the decision to ship an MVP covering Facebook and Instagram first, and expand from there.

03 Competitive Research

What I found when I looked at every tool in the market

Before touching Figma, I audited how every major tool had approached this problem. The goal wasn't inspiration — it was to understand the actual state of the art, find where the real friction lived, and decide what "doing this well" would mean for SocialPilot's users specifically.

Here's what I found.

Sprout Social

Deep — but only if you can afford it

Sprout has the most comprehensive competitor feature in the market. Cross-network reports, sentiment tracking, share of voice, post-level analysis. But it's gated behind their higher-tier plans, which start significantly above what most small-to-mid agencies pay. The feature exists — most SocialPilot users can't access it.

Hootsuite

Broad — but frustrating to actually use

Hootsuite lets you track up to 20 competitors per network, which sounds generous. But there's a catch that makes it genuinely tedious: you can only compare your brand against one competitor at a time. Tracking five competitors means running the same report five times. The data is there. The workflow to use it isn't.

Socialinsider

The quality benchmark — but it's a separate tool entirely

Socialinsider is a dedicated analytics product covering Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, TikTok, and YouTube. The depth of its competitor reports is unmatched. But it's a standalone tool — agencies using it have to maintain two subscriptions and two workflows. It showed us what thorough looks like. It also showed us the friction our users were living with.

Tool Competitor Tracking Platforms Compare multiple at once Live data Integrated with publishing
Sprout Social ✓ Yes FB, IG, Twitter, LinkedIn ✓ Yes ✗ Scheduled ✓ Yes
Hootsuite ✓ Yes FB, IG, Twitter ✗ One at a time ✗ Scheduled ✓ Yes
Socialinsider ✓ Yes FB, IG, Twitter, TikTok, YouTube ✓ Yes ~ Partial ✗ Standalone only
Buffer ~ Limited FB, IG ✗ No ✗ No ✓ Yes
SocialPilot (before) ✗ None — — — —

ⓘ Based on product audit conducted at the time of the project.

The pattern across all of them: competitor intelligence either lives in a tool agencies can't afford, requires a separate subscription on top of their management tool, or has enough UX friction that the data is technically there but genuinely annoying to extract. Nobody had built something that was both accessible and actually integrated into the workflow where agencies already lived. That was the real opportunity — not features, but integration.

04 Problem Definition

The most important design decision happened before any screen was drawn

The brief was: add competitor benchmarking. The obvious interpretation — a side-by-side comparison view — was exactly what users said they wanted. But as I mapped out what data was actually available, a fundamental problem emerged.

The data asymmetry problem

Your own account

Followers & growth rate
Post reach & impressions
Audience demographics
Engagement rate
Reactions, comments, shares
Posting frequency & trends
Ad performance data
≠ Not equivalent

A competitor's public page

Followers (count only)
Reach & impressions
Audience demographics
Engagement (public posts only)
Reactions, comments, shares
Posting frequency & trends
Ad performance data
⚠ Forcing these into a direct comparison view doesn't show users the truth — it misleads them into making decisions based on false equivalence.

The design decision this forced

Two directions — one was honest, one wasn't.
❌ Explored & rejected

Side-by-side comparison view — visually compelling and immediately satisfying, but built on mismatched data sets. Would require asterisks, caveats, and intentional gaps that erode trust in the feature and in the data.

✓ Chosen direction

A dedicated Competitor Reports section — separate from your own analytics, fully honest about what public data is available, and focused on the insights that are genuinely actionable from competitor pages alone.

The right design decision here wasn't the most obvious one. Choosing not to build a misleading comparison — even when users asked for it — was more honest, more durable, and more respectful of user intelligence.

This reframed the feature's purpose: not "compare yourself to competitors" but "what can users learn from a competitor's public data to make better decisions?" A narrower question — and a cleaner design.

05 Design

Designing the feature

With the problem correctly scoped, I moved into design. The brief from PM and leadership defined three fixed constraints: the feature would live under Analytics in the existing nav, it would use the existing drag-and-drop report builder pattern, and it would cover Facebook and Instagram at launch. Everything else — every screen, every interaction state, every component, every edge case — was mine to figure out.

A note on process: At SocialPilot, we work directly in high-fidelity — iterating rapidly in Figma across layout directions and edge cases until we converge on a solution. The designs shown here are the end of that iteration process, not a first attempt.

Navigation — Feature Entry Point Context
1

Users land on Competitor Reports through the Analytics nav flyout — the same place they go for their own reports and templates. Keeping it here meant users already in the reporting mindset would find it naturally, rather than hunting for it in a separate section of the product.

2

The yellow active state on Analytics persists throughout the feature. When a user is inside Competitor Reports, they still know they're inside Analytics — not a standalone tool, not a detached page. This context matters for agencies who move quickly between their own reports and competitor data.

Competitor Reports — List View Entry Point
1

Agencies don't want to manually send the same report every Monday. The Schedule column shows that each report already has an automated delivery day set. Users discover this while scanning the list — no settings panel to dig through, no feature to learn. It's just there, doing its job.Alternative considered: putting scheduling inside a per-report settings panel. Rejected because agencies need this as a default, not an optional configuration.

2

Many agencies track the same competitor on both Facebook and Instagram. In those rows, the only thing that differs is the platform icon. Making that icon prominent — not a tiny afterthought — means users can scan the list at a glance rather than reading each row carefully.

3

The list stays scannable regardless of how many reports a user has added. With a 10-account limit, most users will have a short list — but agencies managing many client accounts can still navigate it quickly without the page becoming a wall of rows.

Add Competitor — Search & Select Flow

Three states of the same modal: empty, mid-search with results, and selections confirmed as chips.

Add Competitor — Empty State Onboarding
1

Users know how many competitors they can track before they start selecting. The "0/10" counter is visible the moment the modal opens — so nobody reaches the limit and hits an unexpected error. The limit becomes a feature constraint you're aware of, not a wall you walk into.

2

Most users' competitors are on Instagram and Facebook — so the platform limitation doesn't stop them. The note sits at the bottom of the modal as a disclosure, not a warning. For users whose competitors are elsewhere, it's an honest heads-up. Placing it at the very top would feel like a disclaimer that slows down the majority to accommodate the edge case.

Add Competitor — Search Results Onboarding
1

When you type a competitor's name, you see all their accounts at once — Instagram and Facebook together. You're looking for a brand, not a specific platform. Showing everything in one list with platform icons means you can select both accounts in a single search, without switching tabs or repeating the same search twice.Alternative considered: separate Instagram and Facebook tabs in the modal. Rejected because it forced users to make a platform decision before they'd even found what they were looking for.

2

When multiple accounts share the same brand name, the page ID helps users pick the right one. Brand names are common and often duplicated across regional pages. Rather than hiding this complexity, the results show enough information — platform, handle, and ID — for users to confidently select the right account.

Add Competitor — Selections Made Onboarding
1

Once you select a competitor, their name appears right inside the search field as a removable tag. You can see exactly what you've added, remove anything with a single click, and keep searching — all in the same interaction space. No scrolling up to check a separate "selected" list.

2

The Add button only becomes active once you've selected at least one account. An empty submission doesn't make sense here — the disabled state communicates that without any error message. When the button turns blue, it's a clear signal that you're ready to proceed.

Competitor Report — View Core Experience
1

The competitor's logo and name appear before any numbers. When an agency is tracking 8 or 10 competitors, it's easy to lose track of whose data you're looking at mid-scroll. Leading with the brand identity means every section is unambiguous — you know you're looking at Burger King's data before you read a single metric.

2

All of a competitor's platforms are visible in one scroll — no tab switching, no separate pages. An agency comparing a brand's Facebook performance against their Instagram presence can see both without navigating away. "Facebook Profile" is a section within the report, not a destination you navigate to.

3

Trend percentages only appear on metrics where they tell you something useful. Posts, Engagements, and Reactions show how they've moved in the last 3 months — that's actionable. Followers don't show a trend because follower growth is slow and a percentage delta over 3 months would look deceptively small. Showing it would create noise, not insight.The deliberate choice: consistency in card layout is less important than accuracy in what each card communicates.

Edit Report — Block-based Customisation Power Feature
1

The report builder lets users show clients exactly what matters — nothing more. An agency reporting on a food brand doesn't need the same metrics as one reporting on a tech startup. Users can drag in the blocks relevant to their client and remove what isn't. The brief specified using SocialPilot's existing builder; my job was deciding which competitor metrics could live inside it and which ones couldn't. Impression and reach blocks, for example, were excluded — those only exist in a managed account's private data, not a competitor's public page. Showing them would have been misleading.The challenge: a pattern built for your own account data doesn't automatically work for public competitor data. Every block had to be evaluated for whether it told the truth about a competitor's page.

2

The right panel groups metrics under each competitor's name. If you're tracking Burger King and McDonald's, their metrics are listed separately — not mixed together in one flat list. When you want to add Burger King's engagement data, you look under Burger King. This stays fast and obvious even as the list of tracked competitors grows.

3

Live Data means the numbers you're looking at are current — not from yesterday's scheduled report. Every other tool in the market refreshes competitor data on a schedule. SocialPilot's Live Data button fetches the latest figures in real time. For agencies building a client presentation or reacting to a competitor's recent campaign, that timeliness matters.

Metrics available at launch

All metrics are derived from publicly available data on the competitor's Facebook and Instagram pages.

👥 Followers
📝 Posts
💬 Engagements
❤️ Reactions
💭 Comments
↗️ Shares
📅 Publishing Trend
📊 Engagement Trend
06 Outcome

What shipped, what it meant, and what I don't know yet

Competitor Reports shipped as a focused MVP — Instagram and Facebook, up to 10 tracked competitors, with a fully customisable block-based report editor, scheduled automated delivery, and Live Data refresh.

0→1 Agencies went from having no competitor visibility inside SocialPilot, to tracking up to 10 competitors — all within the tool they already used for everything else
Removed The need to open separate tools like Socialinsider or manually check competitor pages — competitive context was now part of the reporting workflow, not outside it
1st To ship live competitor data refresh in the social media management space — every competing tool still relied on scheduled snapshots at the time of launch

Strategic impact

Competitor Reports removed a recurring objection in sales conversations — agencies evaluating SocialPilot against Sprout Social and Hootsuite had cited the analytics gap directly. Live Data refresh, which no competitor had shipped at the time, gave SocialPilot a differentiated talking point rather than just feature parity.

A note on metrics

We didn't have feature-level instrumentation in place at launch to measure adoption rate, engagement with the report editor, or the impact on churn. In retrospect, I would have advocated for success metrics to be defined before the feature shipped — specifically: what percentage of active analytics users create at least one competitor report within 30 days, and does the feature correlate with reduced churn among agency accounts. I'm working with the PM to pull these numbers retrospectively and will update this case study when available.

07 Reflection

What I'd do differently

Honest retrospective

Two mistakes. Two directions I'd push further.

01
Mistake

We skipped user testing entirely — and it showed

We went from design to build without a single usability test. Post-launch, users were confused about why some search results were selectable and others weren't — private accounts appear in search but can't be tracked, and nothing in the UI explained that. A 30-minute session with two or three users would have surfaced this before it became a support issue.

02
Mistake

I didn't define success metrics before the feature shipped

There was no instrumentation plan, no agreed definition of "good adoption," no baseline. Post-launch, we had qualitative signals but no quantitative story. For a feature built to address churn risk, that's a significant gap. Outcome definition should be part of the design brief, not an afterthought.

03
Would push further

The comparison problem deserves a real design solution, not an avoidance

The decision to avoid direct comparison was correct given the data asymmetry — but we stopped short of solving the real problem. There's a version where your own public-equivalent metrics sit alongside a competitor's, with clear labelling of what's comparable. Transparent data framing is better design than data avoidance.

04
Would push further

Discoverability was an afterthought

The feature lives in a nav flyout — invisible to users who don't explore the menu. I'd design a contextual entry point inside the main Analytics dashboard: a prompt when viewing your own metrics that says "See how competitors compare." A feature users don't discover doesn't have impact.

SocialPilot — Competitor Reports

Product Design Case Study · Instagram & Facebook Competitor Analytics
